title: Arrays
summary: Bash supports indexed arrays (numeric keys, zero-based) and associative arrays (string keys). They are the primary way to store collections of values in a script without resorting to external tools.
keyPoints: Indexed array: arr=(a b c) or declare -a arr
Iterate: for item in "${arr[@]}"; do ... done

exercises.prompt: How do you get the number of elements in an array named arr?
exercises.explanation: The # prefix inside ${ } means 'length of'. [@] expands all indices.

exercises.prompt: Complete the line to append the value 'purple' to an existing array called colors.
exercises.explanation: += with parentheses appends one or more elements to an indexed array.

exercises.prompt: Declare an associative array called 'capitals' mapping 'France' to 'Paris' and 'Japan' to 'Tokyo'. Then print each key and value on one line.
exercises.hint: Use declare -A, then loop over ${!capitals[@]} to get keys.
exercises.solution:
declare -A capitals
capitals[France]=Paris
capitals[Japan]=Tokyo
for country in "${!capitals[@]}"; do
  echo "$country: ${capitals[$country]}"
done
